sourceText: I was born in the same year as Napoleon, and I too have my ambitions.
This reflects his immense personal ambition and his view of himself as a major historical actor on the world stage, comparable to the greatest European leaders of his time.
The whole of the revenue is mine; and the whole of the expense is mine.
This statement bluntly illustrates his policy of state monopoly over the entire Egyptian economy, which was the financial engine for his military and industrial reforms.
My country is no longer in Africa; we are now part of Europe.
This expresses his core vision of transforming Egypt from a traditional Ottoman province into a modern, Westernized state capable of competing with European powers on its own terms.

I faced rigid opposition from traditional religious institutions and conservative scholars. Additionally, I was forced into exile for years due to my political activities amidst the pressure of European colonialism in Egypt.

Instead of direct confrontation, I pursued long-term change through education. I utilized my public office as Grand Mufti to reinterpret Islamic law for modern times, championing reason and rationality to lead gradual societal reform.
I went to the West and saw Islam, but no Muslims; I got back to the East and saw Muslims, but no Islam.

This quote criticizes the state of Muslim societies by observing that the ideal values of Islam—such as order, cleanliness, and honesty—were more visible in Western societies, while Muslims themselves were not living up to these principles.
To truly raise a nation, you must first raise its mothers.

This emphasizes the critical importance of female education. Abduh believed that educated mothers would raise an enlightened next generation, which he saw as the fundamental basis for national progress.
The two books, that of God's revelation and that of God's creation, cannot contradict one another.
